beats.narration: You set the magnetizing field H through ampere-turns; the part answers with flux density B. Their ratio is permeability — and in steel it runs wild.

payload.title: B = μH
payload.sub: You set H. The part's permeability decides everything else.
payload.panelLabel: ET COIL // H IN, B OUT

beats.narration: Permeability is the slope of the B-H curve. At low bias it's huge and unstable; drive to the flat saturated top and it collapses to one.

payload.heading: The slope the coil actually feels
payload.note: LOW BIAS: LARGE, METALLURGY-DEPENDENT ΔB  ·  SATURATED TOP: μΔ ≈ μ₀ (μᵣ ≈ 1)

beats.narration: Unsaturated steel fails twice. Chemistry, hardness, and stress move the dot exactly like flaws, and permeability collapses penetration to hundredths of a millimeter.
Saturation is the cure. A strong DC field parks steel on that flat top, so the AC test field sees permeability one.
So prove it, don't assume it. Raise the DC field until the reference signal stops changing, then hold it constant through the run.

payload.sub: If the DC sags, the bias slides back and the noise returns.
